retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Humerus -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Scapulohumeral muscles: The deltoid muscle, which defines the shoulder contour of the upper limb, originates at three locations: the acromion of the scapula, the spine of the scapula and clavicle, with each part inserting into the deltoid tuberosity of the humerus. The deltoid muscle allows internal/external rotation and abduction/adduction of the humerus. [22] The pectoralis major muscle originates at the clavicle, manubrium, sternum body, and true ribs and inserts into the intertubercular sulcus of the humerus. It allows adduction, flexion, extension, and medial rotation of the humerus. [23] Four muscles form the rotator cuff; the subscapularis, supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and teres minor. The subscapularis muscle originates from the subscapular fossa of the scapula and inserts onto the lesser tubercle of the humerus, facilitating internal rotation of the humerus. The supraspinatus muscle originates in the supraspinous fossa of the scapula and inserts into the greater tubercle of the humerus, facilitating the abduction of the humerus. The infraspinatus muscle originates in the infraspinous fossa and scapula spine and inserts into the greater tubercle of the humerus as well and allows external rotation of the humerus. The teres major originates the inferior angle of the scapula and inserts into the lesser tuberosity of the humerus, allowing internal rotation and adduction. The teres minor originates on the lateral border of the scapula and inserts into the greater tubercle and allows external rotation. [24] [25] [26] [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Humerus -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Anterior compartment muscles: The biceps brachii muscle has a long head and short head but does not have an actual origin or insertion sites on the humerus. [28] However, there is a transverse humeral ligament which projects from the lesser tubercle to the greater tubercle of the humerus and converts the humeral intertubercular groove into a canal which the tendon of the biceps brachii long head travels through on its way from its origination on the supraglenoid tubercle of the scapula to the radius.  The coracobrachialis originates on the coracoid process of the scapula and inserts onto the humerus and its medial surface, allowing flexion and internal rotation. [29] The brachialis muscle originates on the anterior surface of the distal humerus and inserts onto the ulna for flexion of the forearm. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Shoulder Muscles -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The short head of the biceps brachii originates from the coracoid process, and the long head originates from the supraglenoid tubercle, passing through the intertubercular groove of the proximal humerus. The biceps brachii is not actually considered a shoulder muscle, but the tendon of its long head originates on the superior lip of the glenoid labrum.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Triceps Muscle -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The long head originates from the infraglenoid tubercle of the scapula, while both the lateral head and the medial head both originate from the humerus. The three heads converge into a single tendon, which attaches to the proximal portion of the olecranon process (the bony prominence of the elbow) located on the upper portion of the ulna. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Rotator Cuff -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Origin: lateral border of the scapula (below the infraglenoid tubercle) Insertion: greater tuberosity of the humerus, below the infraspinatus tendon.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Muscles -- Muscles -- Intrinsic Muscles of Hand
retrieved_docs.content: Thenar muscles Opponens pollicus Function: Opposition of the thumb Origin: Flexor retinaculum  and tubercle of trapezium Insertion: Lateral aspect of first metacarpal Innervation: Recurrent branch of median nerve (C8, T1) Abdu ctor pollicus brevis Function: Abduction of the thumb at the metacarpophalangeal joint Origin: Flexor retinaculum  and tubercle of scaphoid Insertion: Lateral aspect of proximal phalanx of first finger Innervation: Recurrent branch of median nerve (C8, T1) Flexor pollicus brevis Function: Flexion of the thumb at the metacarpophalangeal joint Origin: Flexor retinaculum  and tubercle of trapezium Insertion: Lateral aspect of proximal phalanx of first finger Innervation: Recurrent branch of median nerve (C8, T1) Adductor Compartment Adductor pollicus Function: Adduction of the thumb Origin: Second, third metacarpal, and capitate Insertion: Proximal phalanx and extensor expansion of 1st finger Innervation: Deep branch of ulnar nerve (C8, T1) Hypothenar Muscles

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Muscles -- Muscles -- Posterior Axioappendicular Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Superficial layer Latissimus dorsi Function: Adduction, medial rotation, extension of humerus Origin: Spinous processes of seventh to 12th thoracic vertebrae, iliac crest, thoracolumbar fascia, and inferior third and fourth rib Insertion: Intertubercular groove of humerus Innervation: Thoracodorsal nerve (C5,C6,C7) Trapezius Function: Elevation, depression, and retraction of the scapula, rotation of glenoid cavity Origin: Superior nuchal line, nuchal ligament, occipital protuberance, spinous processes of C7- T12 Insertion: Spine of scapula, acromion, and lateral clavicle Innervation: CN XI Deep Layer Levator scapulae Function: Adduction, medial rotation, extension of humerus Origin: Transverse processes of C1 through C4 vertebrae Insertion: Scapula at its medial border Innervation: Thoracodorsal nerve (C5, C6, C7) Rhomboid major

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Arm Muscles -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: The biceps brachii: the long head originates from the supraglenoid tubercle of the scapula, and the short head originates from the tip of the coracoid process of the scapula. The insertion of this muscle will be on the posterior part of the radius tuberosity and by the bicipital aponeurosis to the medial aspect of the forearm. It serves as a powerful supinator of the forearm and flexion of the elbow joint and plays a minimal role in flexion of the shoulder. Innervated by the musculocutaneous nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Muscles -- Muscles -- Scapulohumeral (Intrinsic Shoulder Muscles)
retrieved_docs.content: Deltoid Function: Anterior part: flexion and medial rotation of arm, middle part: abduction of arm, posterior part: extension and lateral rotation of arm Origin: Lateral clavicle, acromion and scapular spine Insertion: Deltoid tuberosity Innervation: Axillary nerve (C5, C6) Teres major Function: Adduction and medial rotation of arm Origin: Posterior surface of  scapula at its inferior angle Insertion: Intertubercular groove on its medial aspect Innervation: Lower scapular nerve (C5, C6) Supraspinatus Function: Initiation of arm abduction Origin: Posterior scapula, superior to the scapular spine Insertion: Superior aspect of the greater tubercle Innervation: Suprascapular nerve (C5, C6) Part of rotator cuff muscles Infraspinatus Function: Lateral rotation of arm Origin: Posterior scapula, inferior  to the scapular spine Insertion: Greater tubercle of humerus, between supraspinatus and teres minor insertion Innervation: Suprascapular nerve (C5, C6) Part of rotator cuff muscles

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Infraspinatus Muscle -- Muscles -- Infraspinatus
retrieved_docs.content: Origin: infraspinous fossa Insertion: greater tubercle of the humerus Action: lateral rotation of the humerus, external rotation of the shoulder, scaption Innervation: suprascapular nerve

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Opponens Pollicis Muscle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The opponens pollicis muscle is in the thenar eminence along with three other muscles that make up the thenar eminence. The opponens pollicis muscle is a muscle originates from the tubercle of the trapezium bone and the transverse carpal ligament (flexor retinaculum). The muscle then inserts onto the entire length of the first metacarpal bone. The function of the opponens pollicis muscle is to draw the thumb across the hand and touch the tips of the other digits. When the opponens pollicis muscle contracts it pulls the thumb medially towards the other digits. The medially drawn thumb is made up of flexion, internal rotation, and palmar abduction of the thumb. This combination equates to the opposition of the thumb. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Bone Markings -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Muscles are crucial in forming bone markings, as they exert traction forces on bones at their attachment sites. These forces stimulate bone growth and remodeling, resulting in the development of various protuberances such as crests, trochanters, tuberosities, and tubercles, which serve as muscle attachment points. The bone markings' sizes and shapes indicate the magnitude and direction of the forces applied by the associated muscles. Additionally, articulating features of bones, like facets, condyles, and heads, develop in response to the articulating surfaces between 2 bones, further influenced by muscle action and joint movement. Overall, the interaction between muscles and bones during movement and physical activity shapes the structural characteristics of bones, reflecting functional demands.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Thorax, Muscles -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: Other muscles of the thorax are involved in upper limb movement, which include the pectoralis major and minor, subclavius, and serratus anterior muscles. The pectoralis major originates from the medial half of the clavicle, anterior sternum, first seven costal cartilages, and aponeurosis of the external oblique and inserts on the lateral lip intertubercular sulcus of the humerus. It functions to flex, adduct, and medially rotate the arm at the glenohumeral joint. Its clavicular head causes flexion of the extended arm while its sternoclavicular head causes extension of the flexed arm. [4] The pectoralis minor muscle originates from the anterior surfaces of ribs 3 to 5 and the deep fascia overlying the related intercostal spaces and inserts on the coracoid process of the scapula. It functions mainly to depress the tip of the shoulder and protract the scapula. It may also assist respiratory efforts as an accessory muscle by lifting the third, fourth, and fifth ribs during inspiration. [2] The subclavius muscle originates at the costochondral junction of the first rib and inserts at the subclavian groove of the clavicle. It functions to stabilize the clavicle. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Supratrochlear -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the human body, many pulley-like structures are known as a trochlea; for example, the medial part of an articular area at the lower end of the humerus is called the trochlea. A ligamentous structure attached to the hyoid bone acts as pully for the digastric muscle. [1] The superior surface of the talus is also known as the trochlear surface. [2] In the ocular orbit, trochlea is a fibrous cartilaginous pully like structure on the nasal aspect of the frontal bone through which superior oblique muscle passes. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Hand Carpal Bones -- Muscles
retrieved_docs.content: Some carpal bones serve as sites of origin or as sites of insertion for the extrinsic and intrinsic muscles of the hand. The flexor carpi ulnaris is the only extrinsic muscle that inserts onto carpal bones, specifically the pisiform and hook of the hamate and the base of the fifth metacarpal bone, which allows it to flex and adduct the wrist joint. All the intrinsic muscles have their origin sites on the carpal bones. The thenar muscles, which include the opponens pollicis, abductor pollicis brevis, and flexor pollicis brevis, have origin sites that involve the prominent tubercles of the scaphoid and trapezium. The adductor pollicis originates from the capitate and the second and third metacarpal bones. Last, the hypothenar muscles, such as the abductor digiti minimi, flexor digiti minimi brevis, and opponens digiti minimi, originate from the pisiform and hook of the hamate. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Shoulder and Upper Limb, Muscles -- Muscles -- Scapulohumeral (Intrinsic Shoulder Muscles)
retrieved_docs.content: Teres minor Function: Lateral rotation of arm Origin: Posterior surface of scapula at its inferior angle Insertion: Inferior aspect of the greater tubercle Innervation: Axillary nerve (C5, C6) Part of rotator cuff muscles Subscapularis Function: Adduction and medial rotation of the arm Origin: Anterior aspect of scapula Insertion: Lesser tubercle of humerus Innervation: Subscapular nerves (C5, C6, C7) Part of rotator cuff muscles *Rotator cuff muscles: supraspinatus, infraspinatus, teres minor, subscapularis

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2234
retrieved_docs.content: Other disorders of synovium, tendon, and bursa—conditions affecting the connective tissues that line joints, support tendons, and cushion bursae—are related to muscle disorders (اضطراب العضلات), highlighting an interconnection between joint‑associated soft‑tissue pathologies and muscular dysfunction.

Together, these entities and relationships illustrate a structured framework that assists clinicians in selecting appropriate diagnostic, surgical, or injection‑based treatments for tendon and tendon‑sheath conditions of the hand and other musculoskeletal sites.

Related incision‑based interventions—aspiration of the bursa of the hand, aspiration of the fascia of the hand (an inverse‑isa of Procedure on musculoskeletal system), division of the joint capsule of the hand or wrist, and drainage of a tendon of the hand—are grouped within “Injections and aspirations of muscles; tendons; bursa; joints and soft tissue.” Division of the joint capsule is an isa of both hand and wrist capsule divisions and is linked to the broader “Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage,” which parents “Incision and excision of joint structures” and the overall “Operation on musculoskeletal system.” Incision and excision of joint structures

Diagnostic and exploratory procedures on tendons and tendon sheaths are organized under the umbrella “Operation on musculoskeletal system” (synonymous with “Procedure on musculoskeletal system”). “Diagnostic procedure on tendon sheath, NOS” and “Diagnostic procedure on tendon, NOS” are child

This framework supports precise anatomical localization, differential diagnosis, and therapeutic planning, especially the central role of nerve decompression procedures across multiple anatomical sites.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1913
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration of bursa of hand is a medical procedure that is classified within the broader category of injections and aspirations of muscles, tendons, bursa, joints, and soft tissue.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 223
retrieved_docs.content: Hand and wrist incisional surgeries are organized under the “Hand, Wrist: Incision” category, which is nested within both “other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” and “OPERATIVE PROCEDURES ON THE UPPER EXTREMITY: WRIST AND HAND.” Specific procedures in this group include aspiration of the bursa of hand, aspiration of fascia of hand, division of the joint capsule of hand, division of the joint capsule of wrist, drainage of tendon of hand, puncture of the bursa of hand, release for tenosynovitis of the abductor pollicis longus, release for tenosynovitis of the extensor (external) pollicis brevis, and release of the tendon sheath of the hand. Aspiration of the bursa of hand is also classified under “Injections and aspirations of muscles; tendons; bursa; joints and soft tissue,” reflecting its minimally invasive fluid‑removal role, while aspiration of fascia of hand is listed as a “Procedure on musculoskeletal system,” emphasizing its musculoskeletal operative relevance. The

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1856
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnostic procedure on tendon sheath, NOS is a medical examination that is performed on the tendon sheath.

retrieved_docs.content: hand, division of the joint capsule of the hand, division of the joint capsule of the wrist, drainage of the tendon of the hand, puncture of bursa of hand, release for tenosynovitis of the abductor pollicis longus, and release for tenosynovitis of the extensor pollicis brevis—are organized as child entities of “Hand, Wrist: Incision.” This node resides within “Injections and aspirations of muscles; tendons; bursa; joints and soft tissue” and under “OPERATIVE PROCEDURES ON THE UPPER EXTREMITY: WRIST AND HAND.” “Division of joint capsule” is a generic ISA for both “Division of joint capsule of hand” and “Division of joint capsule of wrist” and is linked to the broader parent “Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage,” itself a child of “Incision and excision of joint structures” and of “Operation on musculoskeletal system.” “Incision and excision of joint structures” is related to “klouby” (bones), emphasizing its bony anatomy relevance. Diagnostic and therapeutic procedures on

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1907
retrieved_docs.content: Removal of rice bodies from tendon sheaths of the hand is regarded as a therapeutic intervention that is closely related to, and possibly synonymous with, other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons. The broader category “other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” functions as a classification that encompasses such specific interventions, including the removal of rice bodies from hand tendon sheaths. This relationship indicates that the hand‑specific procedure is considered part of the general group of muscle and tendon therapies, reflecting its inclusion within the overall taxonomy of musculoskeletal therapeutic techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: of tendon sheath fall under the broader category of other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons. “Release of tendon sheath of hand” is mapped from this same therapeutic group and is recognized as a type of hand surgical operation (العمليات الجراحية في اليد). The procedure “Removal of rice bodies from tendon sheaths of hand” is related to, and possibly synonymous with, the broader “Other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” group. Collectively, these entities illustrate a structured framework that links specific aspiration, diagnostic, exploratory, irrigation, release, and removal actions to their respective procedural categories, anatomical sites, and linguistic equivalents, facilitating coherent understanding and management of musculoskeletal procedures in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1914
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration of fascia of hand is a medical procedure performed on the musculoskeletal system, specifically targeting the fascial tissue of the hand for removal of fluid or other material. This intervention is classified as a procedure on the musculoskeletal system, reflecting its role in diagnosing or treating conditions affecting hand fascia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1968
retrieved_docs.content: Musculoskeletal system excision is a broad surgical category that includes the specific procedure of removal of rice bodies from tendon sheath, which is considered a child or subprocedure within this overall excisional approach. This relationship indicates that extracting rice bodies—a manifestation often associated with chronic inflammatory conditions—from a tendon sheath is classified under the larger umbrella of musculoskeletal system excision techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 150
retrieved_docs.content: Aspiration of the bursa of the hand, aspiration of the fascia of the hand, division of the joint capsule of the hand, division of the joint capsule of the wrist, drainage of the tendon of the hand, puncture of the bursa of the hand, and releases for tenosynovitis of the abductor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis are grouped within the “Hand, Wrist: Incision” subcategory, which itself resides under the broader “other therapeutic procedures on muscles and tendons” and the operative classification “OPERATIVE PROCEDURES ON THE UPPER EXTREMITY: WRIST AND HAND.” Division of joint capsule is a specific form of “Division of joint capsule of hand” or “Division of joint capsule of wrist” and is a child of the more general class “Division of joint capsule, ligament, or cartilage,” which is linked to both “Incision and excision of joint structures” and the overarching “Operation on musculoskeletal system” (synonymous with “Procedure on musculoskeletal system”). “Operation on

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1858
retrieved_docs.content: Exploration of tendon sheath is a surgical procedure that is classified both as a musculoskeletal system incision and as a procedure on tendon sheath. As a musculoskeletal system incision, it involves cutting into tissues of the musculoskeletal region to gain access to the tendon sheath. As a procedure on tendon sheath, it specifically targets the sheath surrounding a tendon for diagnostic evaluation or therapeutic intervention. By being a parent category for both types of actions, the exploration of tendon sheath underscores its dual role in orthopedic surgery: providing direct access to tendon sheaths through an incision and performing specialized manipulations within the sheath itself. This integrated classification aids clinicians in understanding the procedural context and selecting appropriate surgical techniques for tendon sheath disorders.
